title: Isomonodromic Deformations of Flat Bundles and Codimension of Hodge Loci
speaker: Hank Morris
abstract: We give a lower bound on the codimension of a component of the non-abelian Hodge locus within a leaf of the isomonodromy foliation on the relative de Rham moduli space of flat vector bundles on an algebraic curve. The bound follows from a more general bound on the rank of a flat vector bundle for which the Hodge filtration persists through the isomonodromic deformation. We will show how this latter bound can be extended to apply to flat vector bundles that do not underlie a variation of Hodge structures, so that it generalizes a result of Landesman-Litt.

proof_stages.label: Nonabelian Hodge setup and Hodge locus
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up curves, flat vector bundles, the relative de Rham moduli space, the Riemann-Hilbert and nonabelian Hodge correspondences, variations of Hodge structure, and the nonabelian Hodge locus of integral variations.

proof_stages.label: Isomonodromic formulation and main codimension theorem
proof_stages.summary: Rigidity of integral monodromy forces the Hodge locus to be studied along isomonodromic deformations, leading to the codimension lower bound for VHS of level l and the related monodromy Lie-algebra bound.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to Simpson filtrations on arbitrary flat bundles
proof_stages.summary: Simpson's theorem supplies any flat bundle with a Griffiths-transverse filtration whose associated graded Higgs bundle is semistable, allowing the VHS statement to be reduced to a rank bound for Simpson filtrations, with remarks on semistability, punctures, and isomonodromic leaves.

proof_stages.label: Deformation-theoretic obstruction map
proof_stages.summary: Atiyah's deformation sequence and the deformation of the filtration produce an obstruction map from tangent directions of the curve to endomorphisms breaking the filtration, whose image in cohomology has dimension controlled by the codimension delta.

proof_stages.label: Griffiths-transverse Higgs factorization and semistable quotient
proof_stages.summary: Griffiths transversality restricts the obstruction to one-step filtration-breaking endomorphisms, and Serre duality plus Higgs-field integrability factor the dual map through a quotient Q, which is then reduced to a semistable bundle with positive slope.

proof_stages.label: Non-GGG inequality for the quotient
proof_stages.summary: Riemann-Roch gives a lower bound and vector-bundle Clifford theory gives an upper bound for sections of the kernel of the map from Q to omega squared, forcing the rank of Q to exceed g minus delta.

proof_stages.label: AM-GM conversion to the rank and codimension bounds
proof_stages.summary: The lower bound on a one-step graded endomorphism piece is converted, via AM-GM/Cauchy-Schwarz estimates over the graded pieces of the filtration, into the stated bound involving the rank of E, the level l, and the codimension delta.

proof_stages.label: Monodromy Lie-algebra variant
proof_stages.summary: Replacing End(E) by the Lie algebra of the algebraic monodromy group, viewed as a sub-Hodge structure of endomorphisms, lets the same obstruction and stability argument prove the announced rank bound for that Lie algebra.

examples.name: Classical Hodge decomposition analogy
examples.note: Classical Hodge decomposition and Hodge classes on ordinary cohomology are used as an analogy to make the nonabelian Hodge correspondence and its Hodge locus concrete, not to derive the theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The classical Hodge decomposition is invoked only as an analogy for the nonabelian Hodge correspondence, with no computation or construction carried out on it.

examples.name: Low-rank flat bundle question
examples.note: An audience question about rank-two or low-rank bundles clarifies how the level assumption in the Simpson-filtration rank bound should be read.
examples.engagement_reason: In response to the rank-two/three question, the speaker checks how the stated inequality behaves in low rank, clarifies the positive-length assumption, and derives the semistability consequence for small rank.

examples.name: Hyperelliptic locus as possible codimension test
examples.note: The hyperelliptic locus in the moduli space of curves is suggested, in response to a question, as a potential place to investigate whether the codimension-g limitation reflects real geometry.
examples.engagement_reason: The hyperelliptic locus is suggested only as a possible place to test codimension phenomena, with no calculation or verification performed on it.

passes.reason: The hyperelliptic locus is suggested as a special test locus whose curves have minimal gonality.
